Detects registry modifications related to the proxy configuration of the system, potentially associated with the Raspberry Robin malware, as seen in campaigns running in Q1 2024.

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: The sample is the MEMZ trojan, a destructive malware known for its unique payloads. Upon user confirmation through a series of warnings, it overwrites the Master Boot Record (MBR) by writing to `\\\\.\\PhysicalDrive0`, which renders the system unbootable. It then launches numerous threads to execute a variety of visual and auditory 'payloads', including screen distortions (`BitBlt`, `StretchBlt`), erratic cursor movement (`SetCursorPos`), opening random websites, and playing system sounds. The malware also implements a watchdog (`/watchdog`) that enumerates processes and triggers a system crash via `NtRaiseHardError` or `ExitWindowsEx` if its own processes are terminated, as an anti-tampering measure. The strings explicitly identify the malware as "MEMZ TROJAN" and detail its destructive purpose.

Detects changes to the "Default" property for keys located in the \Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\App Paths\ registry. Which might be used as a method of persistence

First, to map an application's executable file name to that file's fully qualified path.
Second, to pre-pend information to the PATH environment variable on a per-application, per-process basis.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature fires on the presence of Base64 encoded URI prefixes (http:// and https://) across any file. The simple presence of such strings is not inherently an indicator of malicious content, but is worth further investigation.

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: This is a highly destructive trojan, a variant of the well-known MEMZ malware, as indicated by strings such as "WaffMEMZ 1.0" and explicit warnings like "THIS IS A VIRUS". Its primary payload is overwriting the Master Boot Record (MBR) by writing to `\\.\PhysicalDrive0`. The malware also employs a watchdog process (`/watchdog`) that enumerates its own instances and forces a system reboot (`ExitWindowsEx`) if any are terminated. Additionally, it launches multiple threads to execute a series of disruptive payloads, including opening random websites, moving the mouse cursor, causing visual screen artifacts (`BitBlt`, `StretchBlt`), playing sounds, and displaying numerous fake error messages.

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: This sample is a variant of the destructive MEMZ trojan. It displays warning messages identifying itself as malware before proceeding. Its primary malicious behavior is overwriting the Master Boot Record (MBR) by writing to `\\.\PhysicalDrive0`, which renders the system unbootable. It also creates a file named `note.txt` with a taunting message, "YOUR COMPUTER HAS BEEN FUCKED BY THE MEMZ TROJAN." Additionally, it implements a watchdog mechanism (`sub_40114a`) that enumerates processes and triggers a system crash via `NtRaiseHardError` or a forced reboot (`ExitWindowsEx`) if its own processes are terminated. The trojan also launches numerous threads to execute disruptive payloads, including screen distortions (`BitBlt`, `StretchBlt`), random cursor movements (`SetCursorPos`), playing sounds (`PlaySoundA`), and opening websites (`ShellExecuteA`).

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: This sample is a variant of the MEMZ Trojan, specifically the 'Clean Version' which focuses on non-destructive but highly disruptive payloads. It utilizes GDI manipulation (BitBlt, StretchBlt) to glitch the display, audio APIs (waveOutWrite) to generate noise, and ShellExecuteA to automatically open various URLs (e.g., google.co.ck, pcoptimizerpro.com). It also hooks user input to move the cursor (SetCursorPos) and send random keystrokes (SendInput). The binary explicitly identifies itself in strings as 'MEMZ' and warns of 'semi-harmful' payloads that can cause data loss, characteristic of demonstration malware or joke programs.

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: The sample is identified as the MEMZ destructive trojan. It exhibits several high-severity malicious behaviors: (1) Overwrites the Master Boot Record (MBR) by opening the raw device '\\.\PhysicalDrive0' and writing a custom bootloader, rendering the OS unbootable. (2) Implements a 'watchdog' mechanism via 'sub_401cba' that monitors its own processes and triggers a system crash or reboot ('sub_401aee') if they are terminated. (3) Executes numerous intrusive GDI payloads ('BitBlt', 'StretchBlt') and audio payloads ('waveOutWrite') to disrupt system use. (4) Modifies system privileges ('SeShutdownPrivilege') to force reboots and uses 'NtRaiseHardError' to induce Blue Screens of Death (BSOD). (5) Opens multiple browser tabs to various URLs and drops a 'note.txt' file as a ransom/manifesto.

sigma_analysis_results.rule_author: Florian Roth (Nextron Systems), Max Altgelt (Nextron Systems), Tim Shelton

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Wow6432Node CurrentVersion Autorun Keys Modification
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects modification of autostart extensibility point (ASEP) in registry.
sigma_analysis_results.rule_author: Victor Sergeev, Daniil Yugoslavskiy, Gleb Sukhodolskiy, Timur Zinniatullin, oscd.community, Tim Shelton, frack113 (split)

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Process Creation Using Sysnative Folder
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects process creation events that use the Sysnative folder (common for CobaltStrike spawns)

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Potential Dropper Script Execution Via WScript/CScript
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects wscript/cscript executions of scripts located in user directories
sigma_analysis_results.rule_author: Margaritis Dimitrios (idea), Florian Roth (Nextron Systems), oscd.community, Nasreddine Bencherchali (Nextron Systems)

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Startup Folder File Write
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: A General detection for files being created in the Windows startup directory. This could be an indicator of persistence.

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Potentially Suspicious Desktop Background Change Via Registry
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description:
Detects registry value settings that would replace the user's desktop background.
This is a common technique used by malware to change the desktop background to a ransom note or other image.

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Disable Internal Tools or Feature in Registry
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects registry modifications that change features of internal Windows tools (malware like Agent Tesla uses this technique)

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: CurrentVersion Autorun Keys Modification
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects modification of autostart extensibility point (ASEP) in registry.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs
